2026 · arXiv · Agents

SWE Atlas: Benchmarking Coding Agents Beyond Issue Resolution

Coding benchmarks have quietly equated "software engineering" with "closing GitHub issues." SWE Atlas builds 284 expert-authored tasks across the work that gets left out — answering questions about a codebase, writing tests, and refactoring — and grades them on engineering quality, not just whether the suite turns green.

Mohit Raghavendra, Soham Dan, Miguel Romero Calvo, … Brad Kenstler, Yunzhong He · Scale AI
venue arXiv citations 0 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-06-18
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

The dominant coding benchmark — SWE-bench and its harder descendants like SWE-Bench Pro and Terminal-Bench — has trained the field to treat software engineering as functional resolution: here is a bug or a feature, produce a patch, run the hidden test suite, count it as solved if the tests pass. That paradigm is clean, programmatically verifiable, and increasingly saturated. It is also a narrow slice of the job.

Real engineering is mostly the work around the patch. You onboard into an unfamiliar repository and trace why something misbehaves at runtime. You write tests that anticipate the edge cases a feature could break. You refactor scattered logic into a clean module without changing a single observable behavior. None of these are "fix the failing test," and the authors argue that optimizing exclusively for issue resolution risks producing agents that are excellent patchers but poor engineers — able to ship a fix yet careless about code health, regressions, and maintainability.

Two things make these categories hard to benchmark, which is exactly why prior work skipped them. First, they resist programmatic verification: there is no single hidden test that tells you whether a Q&A answer is comprehensive or whether a refactor is clean. Second, the existing attempts that do exist are thin — codebase-Q&A benchmarks (e.g. CoReQA) are answerable by static reading of the source, and test-writing benchmarks largely grade whether a single bug-reproduction test fires. SWE Atlas argues the missing measurement is engineering quality under realistic, under-specified conditions, and sets out to build it.

// how it works

The method

SWE Atlas is 284 expert-authored tasks drawn from 18 actively maintained open-source repositories, split across three workflows: Codebase Q&A (124), Test Writing (90), and Refactoring (70). Each workflow is broken into sub-categories — Q&A spans architecture & system design (44), root-cause analysis (37), code onboarding (28), security (11) and API integration (4); Test Writing spans integration (44), unit (41) and acceptance (5) tests; Refactoring spans decomposition (29), interface evolution (20), extraction (14) and relocation (7).

The first deliberate design choice is under-specification. Unlike SWE-Bench Pro or Terminal-Bench, whose prompts are fully specified and unambiguous, SWE Atlas tasks are written as a developer would actually phrase them — goal-oriented natural language, sometimes vague, often requiring the agent to set up and run the application and reason about live runtime behavior rather than read code statically. The problem statements are net-new: experts rewrote them rather than copying the original GitHub issue.

The second choice is a hybrid verifier. Where a programmatic check exists, SWE Atlas uses it; where it doesn't, it layers on rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge. A rubric is a checklist of self-contained binary checks, each graded independently as a YES/NO on whether the solution exhibits that property. A task passes only when both gates clear:

pass(task) = [ functional check ] ∧ ⋀ i ∈ must-have ( judge(rubrici) = YES ) // every programmatic gate AND every mandatory rubric item must pass — one missed edge case fails the task

The gates are tailored per workflow. Codebase Q&A is rubric-only: "Answer Comprehensiveness" plus "Negative" rubrics, all must-have. Test Writing combines a manifest check (a judge confirms the agent's listed tests match what it added), a mutation check (the agent's tests are run on working code, then re-run after the target code is replaced with a no-op stub — they must pass first and fail second), and rubrics for comprehensiveness, placement and conventions. Refactoring combines a regression check (the project's suite plus hidden tests must not flip pass→fail, and the agent may not touch test files) with rubrics for code maintainability, artifact cleanup, and anti-patterns. On average a task carries 10.5 rubrics for Q&A, 17.1 for Test Writing, and 17.4 for Refactoring, with refactoring tasks backed by ~18 tests each.

Quality control is heavy. Experts author a reference solution that passes all checks, an independent QC team reviews each task, and finally three trusted experts audit every task — any rubric flagged invalid, over-prescriptive, or ambiguous by at least two of the three is removed. Models are run on their native vendor scaffolds (Codex CLI, Claude Code, Gemini CLI) to reproduce the real end-user experience, plus on the minimal mini-SWE-agent (bash-only) scaffold to compare raw model capability stripped of agent tooling.

// what they found

The results

Nobody is close to solving it. On the headline Pass@1 (mean over 3 trials), GPT-5.4 on Codex leads at 43.49% and Opus 4.7 on Claude Code follows at 41.89% — the two are effectively tied under a common scaffold. The best open-weight model, GLM 5, manages only 24.03%, and the weakest entrants land in the mid-teens. Consistency is worse than the averages suggest: Pass3 (all three trials passing) drops 30–50% relative to Pass@1 — even the leaders sit at ~29% Pass3 — so a single sampled success badly overstates reliability.

Model (scaffold)Pass@1Pass3Q&ATestRefac
GPT-5.4 (Codex)43.4929.240.844.444.3
Opus 4.7 (Claude Code)41.8929.240.338.548.6
Opus 4.6 (Claude Code)34.9322.933.336.735.6
Gemini 3.1 Pro (Gemini CLI)25.2313.916.031.233.8
GLM 5 (mini-SWE-agent)24.0311.620.528.724.2
Kimi K2.5 (mini-SWE-agent)19.059.913.125.821.0

The most important result isn't the leaderboard — it's the gap between functional correctness and engineering quality. If you graded refactoring only by whether tests still pass, every model would score 60–80%, mirroring the saturation of older benchmarks. Switch to the rubric and scores collapse and spread out, opening a gap of roughly 15–40 points for refactoring and 10–15 points for test writing. Models pass the functional check more often than the rubric for every model and both workflows. The gap concentrates in exactly the categories an engineer would care about: Code Maintainability and Artifact Cleanup for refactors (the structural work is left half-done — dead code, stale imports, missed call sites), and Test Comprehensiveness for tests (top models miss the edge cases).

The failure analysis is where the benchmark earns its keep. On Codebase Q&A, the best models are visibly execution-oriented: higher resolve rate tracks with more code executions, and the top runs spin up the actual application, fire live requests, and read the runtime output. But they still fail in characteristic, vendor-specific ways — GPT models predominantly fail on Missing/Incomplete Information (running experiments but not covering every sub-question), while Claude models fail predominantly on No Runtime Evidence (46%), lapsing into explaining the code statically on tasks that explicitly demanded runtime proof.

Two more diagnostics sharpen the picture. On Test Writing, writing more tests isn't writing good tests — weaker models pad the suite with happy-path tests carrying weak assertions that pass on both the working code and the no-op mutant, while stronger models write fewer, more targeted tests that actually kill the mutation. On Refactoring, performance collapses as the gold patch grows from tens to thousands of lines: the bottleneck is multi-file coordination — frontier models touch more of the required files but still miss call sites — with only a small uptick on the very largest tasks, which turn out to be mechanical mass renames. Even how models work differs sharply: Claude Opus 4.6 spawns sub-agents in 96.5% of Q&A trials (avg 2.64 per trial), while Gemini 3.1 Pro, given the same delegation tool, uses one in ~1% of trials and effectively runs as a single loop.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

This is a genuinely well-instrumented benchmark, and it confronts its two biggest threats head-on rather than hand-waving them. The first is contamination — every refactoring task is anchored to a real merged PR whose gold patch already lives in public git history and likely in pretraining corpora. The authors run their own memorization screen: on passing refactoring trials, mean Jaccard similarity over added lines is just 0.51 (Opus 4.6) and 0.35 (GPT-5.4), with diff-sequence similarities of 0.68 / 0.61 — well short of the lump near 1.0 that verbatim regurgitation would produce. They also audited tool calls for live solution lookup and found only 2 runs searching raw.githubusercontent.com on 4 of 210 trials, all of which still failed. That's a credible, falsifiable contamination argument, and notably more transparent than the SWE-Bench Verified situation it cites (OpenAI stopped reporting it; Anthropic now screens-and-excludes flagged subsets).

The second threat is the judge itself. Rubric-based LLM-as-a-judge is only as trustworthy as its consistency, and here the numbers are reassuring: across 1,306 rubric-level judgments, three independent judge models agree at Cohen's κ of 0.74–0.84 and Macro-F1 of 0.87–0.92; a flakiness re-run of 30 Q&A responses five times reached unanimous verdicts on 99.2% of 510 items (avg κ 0.983). The authors credit their insistence on atomic, self-contained binary rubric items — a sound design principle that genuinely seems to be paying off in reproducibility.

Where I'd push back: the inter-judge agreement establishes that the judges agree with each other, not that they agree with ground-truth engineering judgment. Three frontier LLMs can be consistently wrong in the same direction — sharing the same blind spots and stylistic preferences — and "atomic rubric" framing reduces variance without guaranteeing validity; a human-vs-judge agreement number would close that loop. The benchmark is also small per cell (70 refactoring, 90 test, 124 Q&A tasks) with Pass@1 confidence intervals around ±3 points, so several leaderboard adjacencies (GPT-5.4 vs Opus 4.7; the open-weight pack) are within noise. Every task is single-turn by construction — real onboarding and refactoring are iterative — and the headline comparison mixes native vendor scaffolds with the model under test, so a chunk of the GPT-5.4-vs-Opus margin is Codex-vs-Claude-Code, not GPT-vs-Claude. Finally, "no clear evidence of leakage" is appropriately hedged, not a clean bill of health.

baselines

Current and strong — GPT-5.4, Opus 4.7, Gemini 3.1 Pro on first-party scaffolds, plus top open-weight models (GLM 5, Kimi K2.5, MiniMax M2.5) under a common mini-SWE-agent harness. Fair and contemporary.

contamination

Directly tested. Passing-solution-vs-gold similarity (Jaccard 0.35–0.51) and a tool-call audit (4/210 trials looked up source, all failed) argue against memorization. Hedged honestly, not proven absent.

what's missed

Judge agreement is judge-vs-judge, not judge-vs-human; small per-category n with ~±3pt CIs blurs close ranks; single-turn only; native-scaffold mixing conflates model and harness.

would raise confidence

A human-expert vs. LLM-judge agreement study, model-vs-model under one fixed scaffold to isolate capability, and larger task counts (or pre-registered CIs) for the close comparisons.

// bottom line

The cleanest contribution here isn't a leaderboard — it's a measurement instrument that makes "engineering quality" tractable and shows it lagging functional correctness by 15–40 points where it's been invisible. The contamination and judge-consistency work is unusually rigorous for a benchmark paper; the open question is whether consistent judges are correct judges, not just agreeing ones.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. "Solving issues" ≠ "engineering." SWE Atlas carves out the work issue-resolution benchmarks ignore — Codebase Q&A, Test Writing, Refactoring — across 284 expert-authored tasks in 18 real repos, and frontier models top out around 42–43% Pass@1.
  2. Quality lags correctness by a wide, measurable margin. Grade refactors by tests alone and everyone scores 60–80%; grade by rubric and the field collapses, opening a 15–40 point gap in maintainability and cleanup — proof that test-pass metrics hide where agents are weak.
  3. The hard parts are runtime reasoning and cross-file coordination. Strong Q&A means actually running the app for live evidence; refactoring performance falls apart on multi-file edits where models miss call sites — and contamination/judge-consistency checks are rigorous enough to make those conclusions credible.
